Pregabalin:
"routes of administration": ["oral"],
    "sites of action": "central nervous system, peripheral nervous system",
    "distribution of targets": ["nervous system"],
    "structural features": "Pregabalin is a chiral molecule (S-enantiomer active), highly hydrophilic (low lipophilicity, logP ~ -1.3), zwitterionic at physiological pH due to carboxylic acid and primary amine functional groups; lack of significant metabolism contributes to low drug-drug interaction risk but its hydrophilicity limits blood-brain barrier penetration efficiency and may contribute to dose-dependent CNS side effects (e.g., dizziness, somnolence); no reactive metabolites formed.",
    "metabolic pathways": "Pregabalin undergoes negligible hepatic metabolism; it is excreted predominantly unchanged in urine via the urinary system; no cytochrome P450 enzymes or phase I/II metabolic enzymes are significantly involved; renal clearance is the primary elimination pathway."
Fentanyl:
"routes of administration": ["intravenous", "intramuscular", "subcutaneous", "buccal", "rectal", "nasal", "topical"],
    "sites of action": "central nervous system, peripheral nervous system",
    "distribution of targets": ["nervous system", "immune system"],
    "structural features": "high lipophilicity (facilitates rapid blood-brain barrier penetration), phenylpiperidine scaffold, N-phenyl-N-(1-phenylethyl)propionamide structure, chiral center (active enantiomer is (R)-fentanyl), metabolites including norfentanyl (inactive but potentially cardiotoxic in high concentrations)",
    "metabolic pathways": "primarily hepatic metabolism via cytochrome P450 enzymes (CYP3A4 and CYP3A5), producing norfentanyl (N-dealkylation) as the major inactive metabolite; minor pathways include hydroxylation and glucuronidation; metabolites excreted renally"
Pramipexole:
"routes of administration": "oral",
    "sites of action": "basal ganglia, substantia nigra, and striatum within the central nervous system",
    "distribution of targets": "nervous system; specifically dopamine D2 and D3 receptors in the basal ganglia, striatum, limbic system, and hypothalamus",
    "structural features": "high selectivity for D3 receptors over D2 receptors; chiral structure with (S)-enantiomer being pharmacologically active; low molecular weight and moderate lipophilicity enabling blood-brain barrier penetration; lack of significant hepatic metabolism leading to renal excretion of unchanged drug increases risk of accumulation and side effects (e.g., hallucinations, impulse control disorders, somnolence) in renal impairment; no reactive functional groups or known toxic metabolites",
    "metabolic pathways": "nervous system, urinary system; minimal hepatic metabolism; primarily excreted unchanged by the kidneys via glomerular filtration and active tubular secretion; no major cytochrome P450 enzymes involved; no significant metabolites formed"
Gabapentin:
"routes of administration": ["oral"],
    "sites of action": "central nervous system",
    "distribution of targets": ["nervous system"],
    "structural features": "Gabapentin is a zwitterionic, highly hydrophilic molecule (log P ≈ -1.4) with low blood-brain barrier permeability despite CNS activity; it contains a primary amine and a carboxylic acid functional group, contributing to its polar nature and reliance on active transport (e.g., LAT1 transporter); it is administered as the (R)-enantiomer but is achiral in practice due to rapid racemization in solution; lack of significant metabolism means parent compound accumulation may contribute to dose-dependent CNS side effects (e.g., dizziness, somnolence, ataxia); no reactive metabolites or structural alerts for direct organ toxicity are present.",
    "metabolic pathways": "Gabapentin undergoes negligible hepatic metabolism; it is not metabolized by cytochrome P450 enzymes or other major metabolic pathways; it is eliminated unchanged primarily by the kidneys via glomerular filtration and active tubular secretion; no significant metabolites are formed; involved system: urinary system; organ: kidneys; enzymes: none."
Citalopram:
"routes of administration": ["oral"],
    "sites of action": "central nervous system",
    "distribution of targets": ["nervous system"],
    "structural features": "Citalopram is a chiral molecule with an S-enantiomer (escitalopram) being the pharmacologically active form; its lipophilicity (logP ~3.5) facilitates blood-brain barrier penetration but contributes to off-target interactions; tertiary amine group and fluorophenyl moiety may contribute to anticholinergic, QT-interval prolongation (via hERG channel inhibition), and serotonin-related side effects; demethylated and didemethylated metabolites retain some pharmacological activity and may contribute to toxicity.",
    "metabolic pathways": "Primarily hepatic metabolism via the cytochrome P450 system, mainly CYP2C19 and CYP3A4 enzymes; major metabolites include desmethylcitalopram (DCT) and didesmethylcitalopram (DDCT); minor metabolism via CYP2D6; metabolites are excreted renally; systems involved: nervous system (site of action), hepatic system (metabolism), urinary system (excretion)."
Fluoxetine:
"routes of administration": ["oral"],
    "sites of action": ["nervous system", "central nervous system (specifically presynaptic neurons in brain regions including raphe nuclei, prefrontal cortex, hippocampus, and basal ganglia)"],
    "distribution of targets": ["nervous system"],
    "structural features": ["high lipophilicity (logP ~4.5), tertiary amine group, chiral center (S-enantiomer is more potent), fluorinated aromatic ring; metabolites such as norfluoxetine (active, long half-life) contribute to prolonged pharmacologic effects and side effects including serotonin-related toxicity (e.g., serotonin syndrome), QT prolongation, and CNS disturbances"],
    "metabolic pathways": ["primarily hepatic metabolism via cytochrome P450 enzymes (CYP2D6 and CYP2C9/19); main pathway: N-demethylation to form active metabolite norfluoxetine; secondary pathways: deamination, oxidation, and glucuronidation; metabolites excreted renally; systems involved: digestive system (first-pass metabolism in liver), urinary system (excretion), nervous system (target organ for metabolite activity)"]
Doxorubicin:
"routes of administration": "intravenous",
    "sites of action": "nucleus of cells in multiple organs including heart, bone marrow, gastrointestinal tract, and solid tumors; primarily affects rapidly dividing cells throughout the body",
    "distribution of targets": "nuclear DNA and topoisomerase II enzymes in cells across the circulatory system, musculoskeletal system, digestive system, urinary system, and reproductive system",
    "structural features": "anthracycline ring structure with quinone and hydroquinone moieties enabling redox cycling; planar aromatic chromophore facilitating intercalation into DNA; high lipophilicity allowing membrane permeability but contributing to cardiotoxicity; stereochemistry (S-configuration at C-7) influences activity and metabolism; formation of reactive oxygen species and semiquinone radicals during redox cycling causes oxidative stress and tissue damage",
    "metabolic pathways": "Primarily metabolized in the liver by carbonyl reductases (e.g., CBR1) to doxorubicinol; minor oxidation by cytochrome P450 enzymes (CYP3A4, CYP2D6); further conjugation via UDP-glucuronosyltransferases (UGTs); metabolites include doxorubicinol (cardiotoxic), aglycones, and glucuronide conjugates; biliary and renal excretion of metabolites"
Paroxetine:
"routes of administration": ["oral"],
    "sites of action": ["nervous system", "central nervous system (specifically presynaptic neurons in brain regions including prefrontal cortex, hippocampus, raphe nuclei)"],
    "distribution of targets": ["nervous system", "central nervous system (serotonin transporters widely distributed in presynaptic neuronal membranes); limited expression of serotonin transporter in peripheral tissues such as gastrointestinal tract (enteric nervous system), platelets, and pulmonary tissue"],
    "structural features": ["chiral molecule (S-enantiomer is pharmacologically active); highly lipophilic (logP ~3.9), facilitating blood-brain barrier penetration but contributing to off-target interactions and anticholinergic-like side effects; fluorophenyl and methylenedioxyphenyl moieties influence metabolic stability and potential for CYP2D6 inhibition; demethylated and glucuronidated metabolites may contribute to toxicity in overdose or with impaired metabolism"],
    "metabolic pathways": ["primarily hepatic metabolism via cytochrome P450 enzymes, especially CYP2D6 (major) and CYP3A4 (minor); involves demethylation to form norparoxetine (active metabolite), followed by oxidation and conjugation (glucuronidation); metabolites excreted renally; systems involved: digestive system (hepatic metabolism), urinary system (renal excretion of metabolites)"]
Escitalopram:
"routes of administration": ["oral"],
    "sites of action": "central nervous system",
    "distribution of targets": ["nervous system"],
    "structural features": "Escitalopram is the S-enantiomer of citalopram; its stereochemistry confers higher selectivity and potency for the serotonin transporter (SERT). Its moderate lipophilicity (log P ~3.4) facilitates blood-brain barrier penetration but contributes to off-target interactions and CNS-related side effects (e.g., insomnia, agitation). The fluorophenyl and phthalane moieties may influence metabolic stability and potential for CYP-mediated drug interactions. The tertiary amine group contributes to basicity (pKa ~10), affecting distribution and lysosomal trapping. No major toxic metabolites are formed, but the desmethyl and didesmethyl metabolites retain weak SERT activity and may contribute to prolonged effects in hepatic impairment.",
    "metabolic pathways": "Primarily metabolized in the liver by cytochrome P450 enzymes, especially CYP2C19 (major) and CYP3A4 (minor); metabolites include S-desmethylcitalopram (S-DCT) and S-didesmethylcitalopram (S-DDCT); further conjugation (glucuronidation) occurs via UGT enzymes; excretion is predominantly renal (as metabolites), with minimal unchanged drug excreted."
Tacrolimus:
"routes of administration": ["oral", "intravenous"],
    "sites of action": "lymphoid tissues (e.g., lymph nodes, spleen), thymus, and peripheral blood T lymphocytes; primarily within the immune system",
    "distribution of targets": "immune system; specifically T lymphocytes in blood, lymphoid organs (spleen, lymph nodes, thymus), and sites of inflammation",
    "structural features": "high lipophilicity (logP ~3–4), macrocyclic lactone structure with multiple chiral centers (12 stereocenters), allylic alcohol and α,β-unsaturated ketone functional groups; metabolites such as 13-O-desmethyl tacrolimus and 31-O-desmethyl tacrolimus exhibit altered immunosuppressive and toxic potential; stereochemical complexity contributes to narrow therapeutic index and interindividual pharmacokinetic variability",
    "metabolic pathways": "primarily hepatic metabolism via cytochrome P450 enzymes (CYP3A4 and CYP3A5) in the digestive system (liver); minor metabolism in intestinal epithelium; major metabolites include 13-O-desmethyl tacrolimus, 31-O-desmethyl tacrolimus, and 15-O-desmethyl tacrolimus; metabolites are excreted via bile into the digestive system and feces, with minimal renal excretion"
